What is working well today with the process?
Basic channel coverage (phone, chat, email) is reliable and stable.
WFM has solid experience in forecasting and scheduling based on historical data.
Telephony platform provides standard metrics (AHT, SL, abandonment) the org understands.
Simple FAQ-type queries are often handled by the current bot, reducing some noise.
Agents are versatile and can handle multiple call types within a queue.
What problems are occurring in the process today?
Crude routing: All claims calls go into one bucket; simple status calls and complex complaints hit the same queue.
Bots add steps instead of removing them — FAQ bot can't act on accounts, so customers repeat themselves to a human.
Intent data is poor due to inconsistent wrap codes; hard to know what customers really call about at scale.
Channels are siloed (voice vs. chat vs. email); no unified view of customer journeys.
Staffing is based on historic patterns and queue-level volume, not on true intent or automation potential.
No systematic way to blend human and AI workload — AI is treated as a bolt-on deflection, not part of planning.

Call Transcript Snippet (Handler asking P&Q for help)

Participants:

  1. Tom (Senior Handler)
  2. Hannah (Policy & Quality Lead)

[00:12] Tom: Hey Hannah, quick one — I’ve got a nontypical water ingress claim. Customer insists they reported damp two months ago, but I don’t see anything logged.

[00:30] Hannah: Is it storm-related or maintenance-related?

Tom: That’s the issue. The surveyor report says: “likely gradual ingress.” So it’s not sudden damage.

Hannah: Okay, that’s a decline then. Did you reference Clause 5.3(b) — “gradual operating causes excluded”?

Tom: Yeah… I think so. But honestly the clause numbering in this product changed last year. The template I’m using says 4.9(c). I’m not sure if it’s the right one.

Hannah: That’s the old template. The SharePoint versions naming is terrible. Use the “2025 Home – Exclusions Pack – Final_v3” folder.

Tom: Found it. Ugh… there are three versions.

Hannah: Yep, that’s what I mean — we need to clean that up. I’ll review your draft to make sure the explanation is consistent.

[01:15] Tom: Thanks. I’m always worried we’ll send something that the Ombudsman says isn’t clear enough.

Hannah: That’s exactly why we want standard building blocks when we move to AI — too much variation today.

FLEXFUND MUTUAL INSURANCE
Internal “Claims Communications Playbook” Excerpt

Claims Communications Playbook v4.2
Section 6: Guidelines for Decline & Partial Settlement Letters

6.1 Mandatory Components

  1. Summary of the customer’s claim in plain language
  2. Facts relied upon (dates, evidence, reports)
  3. Reference to specific policy clause(s)
  4. Clear decision statement (approve/partial/decline)
  5. Explanation of reasoning, proportionate to complexity
  6. Customer’s right to request reconsideration
  7. Complaint rights & time limits
  8. Ombudsman signposting

6.2 Common Issues Identified by Policy & Quality Audits

  1. Missing clause references
  2. Outdated templates being used
  3. Ambiguous explanations (“not covered”) instead of specific reasons
  4. Tone too legalistic or too blunt
  5. Inconsistent instructions for next steps
  6. No documentation in Guidewire showing the rationale

6.3 Known Workarounds

  1. Handlers saving local copies of templates (“v2 – cleaner wording”)
  2. Copying paragraphs from old letters
  3. Editing tone based on personal style

Current State ("As-Is") Process Document

Contact Center Operations – Flex Fund Mutual Insurance

Document Purpose: This document details the existing contact center workflow, actors, tools, and challenges to establish a complete understanding of current operations before AI agent transformation.

Evidence Sources: Interview transcript with Contact Center Operations Manager (Liam Patel), interview notes from discovery sessions, Claims Communications Playbook v4.2, internal collaboration logs, and embedded process documentation.

1.0 Process Overview and Actors

1.1 Process Scope

Flex Fund Mutual Insurance operates a unionized contact center of approximately 200 agents handling customer inquiries across three primary channels: voice, chat, and email. The process encompasses initial customer contact through inquiry resolution or handoff to specialized teams. The organization manages motor and home insurance claims, policy servicing, and billing inquiries through largely manual workflows with minimal automation.

1.2 Primary Actors and Responsibilities

Contact Center Agents (Frontline): ~200 unionized staff responsible for authenticating customers, handling routine inquiries, performing warm transfers, creating async cases, and assigning wrap codes. Agents demonstrate versatility in managing multiple inquiry types within assigned queues.

Claims Handlers and Adjusters: Process claims documentation, review evidence, interpret policy coverage, draft customer communications using the Claims Communications Playbook, and make coverage decisions subject to approval thresholds.

Policy & Quality Lead: Provides guidance on policy clause interpretation, template usage, regulatory compliance standards, reviews handler communication drafts, and interfaces with regulatory bodies.

Specialized Teams: Dedicated resources for claims processing, billing inquiries, and complaint handling. Receive work through warm transfers or async case assignments.

WFM Team: Forecasts contact volume by queue and half-hour interval, builds agent schedules based on historical patterns, performs intraday adjustments, and tracks adherence metrics.

2.0 Stages and Steps ("As-Is" Workflow)

Stage 1: Voice Channel Entry and Routing

Step 1.1 – IVR Menu Presentation

  1. Actor: Automated system
  2. Customer calls main number and hears generic greeting
  3. IVR presents three options: (1) Claims, (2) Policy & Servicing, (3) Billing
  4. Evidence: "Main number → IVR with 3 options"

Step 1.2 – Queue Assignment

  1. Actor: ACD system
  2. Customer selection routes to single queue per line of business
  3. No differentiation between inquiry types (status check vs. complex complaint)
  4. Evidence: "Single queue per IVR option; Limited routing intelligence"

Step 1.3 – Agent Scheduling and Availability

  1. Actor: WFM system
  2. WFM schedules agents by half-hour intervals based on historical volume
  3. No consideration of intent types or automation potential
  4. Evidence: "WFM schedules by half-hour intervals; Staffing based on history not intent"

Step 1.4 – Call Handling

  1. Actor: Contact center agent
  2. Agent authenticates customer identity
  3. Agent manually navigates multiple systems to retrieve information
  4. Agent responds to customer request
  5. Agent assigns wrap code from extensive list
  6. Agent closes interaction
  7. Evidence: "Agent authentication → Request handling → Wrap code assignment → Interaction closure"

Stage 2: Chat Channel Entry and Bot Interaction

Step 2.1 – Chat Initiation

  1. Actor: Customer
  2. Customer opens chat window via website or customer portal
  3. Evidence: "Web/portal chat initiation"

Step 2.2 – Rules-Based Bot Engagement

  1. Actor: FAQ bot
  2. Bot attempts to answer static questions (hours, claim reporting process, required documents)
  3. Bot cannot access account information or perform transactions
  4. Bot provides static content responses only
  5. Evidence: "Rules-based bot for FAQs; Static content responses; Limited account capabilities"

Step 2.3 – Human Escalation

  1. Actor: System escalation logic
  2. Any account-specific or complex query triggers immediate escalation to human agent
  3. Evidence: "Complex/account queries → human"

Step 2.4 – Agent Chat Handling

  1. Actor: Contact center agent
  2. Agent receives minimal context (last few conversation lines)
  3. Agent handles inquiry starting from limited information
  4. Agent applies minimal wrap coding
  5. Evidence: "Agent sees previous bot conversation; Minimal wrap coding; Limited routing intelligence"

Stage 3: Email Channel Management

Step 3.1 – Email Receipt

  1. Actor: Email system
  2. Inbound emails arrive in shared inboxes
  3. No automated triage or prioritization
  4. Evidence: "Shared inboxes; Minimal routing logic"

Step 3.2 – Manual Assignment

  1. Actor: Team lead or agent self-selection
  2. Emails manually assigned to available agents
  3. Basic folder organization used for tracking
  4. Evidence: "Manual assignment; Basic folder organization; No structured workflow"

Stage 4: Claims Letter Drafting (Decline/Partial Settlement)

Step 4.1 – Template Location

  1. Actor: Claims handler
  2. Handler navigates SharePoint to locate appropriate template
  3. Multiple versions exist with ambiguous naming conventions
  4. Handler often asks colleagues via Slack/Teams for correct version
  5. Evidence: Slack log – "Anyone know where the updated 'Motor Wear & Tear Decline' template is? SharePoint is showing five folders."

Step 4.2 – Clause Identification

  1. Actor: Claims handler
  2. Handler identifies relevant policy exclusion clause
  3. Uncertainty exists around clause numbering due to product updates
  4. Handler may reference outdated clause numbers
  5. Evidence: Call transcript – "The clause numbering in this product changed last year. The template I'm using says 4.9(c). I'm not sure if it's the right one."

Step 4.3 – Draft Creation

  1. Actor: Claims handler
  2. Handler drafts letter using located template
  3. Handler edits tone and language based on personal style
  4. Known workarounds include copying paragraphs from old letters and maintaining local template versions
  5. Evidence: Playbook – "Handlers saving local copies of templates ('v2 – cleaner wording'); Copying paragraphs from old letters; Editing tone based on personal style"

Step 4.4 – Quality Review

  1. Actor: Policy & Quality Lead
  2. Quality lead reviews draft for compliance with Communications Playbook
  3. Reviews for correct clause references, clear explanations, appropriate tone
  4. Evidence: Call transcript – "I'll review your draft to make sure the explanation is consistent."

Stage 5: Warm Transfer Handoffs

Step 5.1 – Specialized Need Identification

  1. Actor: Contact center agent
  2. Agent identifies inquiry requiring specialized expertise (complex claim, billing dispute, formal complaint)
  3. Evidence: "Agent identifies specialized need"

Step 5.2 – Live Transfer Execution

  1. Actor: Contact center agent
  2. Agent performs live transfer to claims, billing, or complaints team
  3. Brief verbal context provided during transfer
  4. No structured feedback loop to originating agent on resolution
  5. Evidence: "Live transfer to specialized team; No structured feedback loop"

Stage 6: Async Case Creation

Step 6.1 – Case Documentation

  1. Actor: Contact center agent
  2. Agent creates internal task or case in system
  3. Documents customer request and captured information
  4. Evidence: "Agent creates internal task/case"

Step 6.2 – Specialist Handling

  1. Actor: Specialized team
  2. Specialist handles case asynchronously
  3. Originating agent has limited visibility into resolution progress or outcome
  4. Evidence: "Another team handles asynchronously; Limited visibility into resolution; No routing optimization feedback"

3.0 Challenges and Pain Points

Challenge 1: Crude and Undifferentiated Routing

Description: All claims calls—regardless of complexity—route to a single queue. Simple status inquiries and complex complaints receive identical treatment.

Impacted Steps: Stage 1, Step 1.2 (Queue Assignment); Stage 1, Step 1.4 (Call Handling)

Tools Involved: Contact center platform/ACD, IVR system

Business Impact: Inefficient agent utilization, customer frustration from extended wait times, skilled agents spending time on routine inquiries

Evidence: "Crude routing: All claims calls go into one bucket; simple status calls and complex complaints hit the same queue."

Challenge 2: Bot Adding Steps Instead of Removing Them

Description: The FAQ bot cannot access account information or complete transactions, forcing customers to repeat information when escalated to human agents.

Impacted Steps: Stage 2, Step 2.2 (Rules-Based Bot Engagement); Stage 2, Step 2.4 (Agent Chat Handling)

Tools Involved: Chat platform, FAQ bot framework

Business Impact: Increased customer effort, negative impact on satisfaction scores, no reduction in agent handle time

Evidence: "Bots add steps instead of removing them—FAQ bot can't act on accounts, so customers repeat themselves to a human."

Challenge 3: Poor Intent Data Quality

Description: Inconsistent wrap code application by agents makes it impossible to understand true customer needs at scale or effectively target automation opportunities.

Impacted Steps: Stage 1, Step 1.4 (Call Handling); Stage 2, Step 2.4 (Agent Chat Handling)

Tools Involved: Contact center platform, reporting/analytics layer

Business Impact: Inability to forecast by intent, misaligned automation investments, inaccurate WFM modeling

Evidence: "Intent data is poor due to inconsistent wrap codes; hard to know what customers really call about at scale."

Challenge 4: Siloed Channel Operations

Description: Voice, chat, and email operate independently with no unified customer journey view. Customers repeating themselves across channels encounter agents with no prior interaction history.

Impacted Steps: All channel entry stages (Voice, Chat, Email)

Tools Involved: Contact center platform, chat platform, email servers, Salesforce CRM

Business Impact: Degraded customer experience, operational inefficiency, inability to measure cross-channel journeys

Evidence: "Channels are siloed (voice vs. chat vs. email); no unified view of customer journeys."

Challenge 5: Historical Volume-Based Staffing

Description: WFM schedules agents based on historic queue-level volume patterns without consideration of inquiry intent or automation potential.

Impacted Steps: Stage 1, Step 1.3 (Agent Scheduling and Availability)

Tools Involved: WFM tool

Business Impact: Misaligned capacity planning, inability to model AI/human workload blend, reactive rather than predictive scheduling

Evidence: "Staffing is based on historic patterns and queue-level volume, not on true intent or automation potential."

Challenge 6: Disconnected AI and Human Workload Planning

Description: The FAQ bot is treated as a bolt-on deflection tool rather than an integrated component of workforce planning.

Impacted Steps: Stage 2, Step 2.2 (Rules-Based Bot Engagement)

Tools Involved: Chat platform, FAQ bot, WFM tool

Business Impact: No optimization of AI-human workload distribution, missed efficiency opportunities, inability to measure true automation impact

Evidence: "No systematic way to blend human and AI workload—AI is treated as a bolt-on deflection, not part of planning."

Challenge 7: Document and Template Management Chaos

Description: SharePoint contains multiple template versions with ambiguous naming, no version control workflow, archived files remain accessible alongside current versions, and handlers maintain local copies with inconsistent edits.

Impacted Steps: Stage 4, Step 4.1 (Template Location); Stage 4, Step 4.3 (Draft Creation)

Tools Involved: SharePoint, local file storage

Business Impact: Regulatory compliance risk, inconsistent customer communications, handler confusion and time waste, potential Ombudsman complaints

Evidence: Slack log – "Use the one in '2025 Pack – FINAL' → but avoid the subfolder labeled '_archive'. That one's from 2021." | "Why does SharePoint even let us save those there? Because we have no versioning workflow."

Challenge 8: Outdated Template and Clause References

Description: Product updates change policy clause numbering, but template libraries are not systematically updated. Handlers risk referencing incorrect clauses in customer communications.

Impacted Steps: Stage 4, Step 4.2 (Clause Identification); Stage 4, Step 4.3 (Draft Creation)

Tools Involved: SharePoint, Guidewire Claims Center

Business Impact: Regulatory non-compliance, customer confusion, potential legal exposure, Ombudsman upheld complaints

Evidence: Call transcript – "The clause numbering in this product changed last year. The template I'm using says 4.9(c). I'm not sure if it's the right one."

Challenge 9: Quality Audit Violations in Customer Letters

Description: Despite the Claims Communications Playbook defining eight mandatory components, audits consistently identify missing clause references, outdated templates, ambiguous explanations, inappropriate tone, and lack of documented rationale.

Impacted Steps: Stage 4, Step 4.3 (Draft Creation); Stage 4, Step 4.4 (Quality Review)

Tools Involved: SharePoint templates, Guidewire Claims Center

Business Impact: Regulatory scrutiny, customer complaints, Ombudsman referrals, brand reputation damage

Evidence: Playbook Section 6.2 – "Common Issues: Missing clause references; Outdated templates being used; Ambiguous explanations; Tone too legalistic or too blunt; Inconsistent instructions; No documentation in Guidewire showing rationale"

Challenge 10: No Feedback Loop on Handoffs

Description: When agents warm transfer or create async cases, they receive no structured information about resolution outcomes, preventing learning and continuous improvement.

Impacted Steps: Stage 5, Step 5.2 (Live Transfer Execution); Stage 6, Step 6.2 (Specialist Handling)

Tools Involved: Contact center platform, internal case management system

Business Impact: Repeated mistakes, inability to identify systemic issues, missed coaching opportunities

Evidence: "No structured feedback loop" | "No routing optimization feedback"

Challenge 11: Minimal Context in Chat Escalations

Description: When the bot escalates to a human agent, only the last few conversation lines are visible. Agents lack full customer context, prior interactions, or account history.

Impacted Steps: Stage 2, Step 2.4 (Agent Chat Handling)

Tools Involved: Chat platform, Salesforce CRM

Business Impact: Customer frustration from repeated explanations, longer handle times, inconsistent service quality

Evidence: Interview transcript – "They see: The last few lines of the conversation, and maybe one or two entities the bot captured. But they don't get a full 'customer profile plus journey.'"

Challenge 12: Manual Email Triage with No Workflow Structure

Description: Email inquiries rely on shared inboxes, manual assignment, and basic folder organization. No automated prioritization, SLA tracking, or structured workflow exists.

Impacted Steps: Stage 3, Step 3.1 (Email Receipt); Stage 3, Step 3.2 (Manual Assignment)

Tools Involved: Outlook/email servers, shared inboxes

Business Impact: Inconsistent response times, risk of missed inquiries, inability to measure email channel performance

Evidence: "Shared inboxes; Manual assignment; Basic folder organization; Minimal routing logic; No structured workflow"

4.0 Tools and Artifacts Used

4.1 Contact Center Technology Stack

Contact Center Platform / ACD System

  1. Function: Telephony infrastructure, IVR menu management, queue management, skills-based routing, real-time and historical reporting
  2. Evidence: "Telephony & Contact Routing: Contact center platform/ACD; IVR, queues, skills, reporting"

Chat Platform

  1. Function: Web and portal chat interface, basic FAQ bot framework, escalation logic to human agents
  2. Integration: Website and customer portal
  3. Evidence: "Digital & Chat: Chat platform integrated with website/portal; Simple FAQ bot framework"

FAQ Bot Framework

  1. Function: Rules-based responses to static questions (hours, claim reporting, document requirements)
  2. Limitation: Cannot access account information or complete transactions
  3. Evidence: "Rules-based bot for FAQs; Static content responses; Limited account capabilities"

Workforce Management Tool

  1. Function: Volume forecasting by queue, agent scheduling, adherence tracking, intraday adjustments
  2. Evidence: "WFM tool for forecasting, scheduling, adherence"

BI / Reporting Layer

  1. Function: Dashboards tracking AHT, Service Level (80/20 target), abandonment rate, contact volume
  2. Limitation: No reliable First Contact Resolution tracking due to poor wrap code quality
  3. Evidence: "BI / reporting layer; Dashboards for AHT, SL, volume"

Email Handling System

  1. Function: Outlook or similar email servers, shared inbox management, basic filtering rules
  2. Evidence: "Email Handling: Outlook / email servers; Shared inboxes and basic rules"

Internal Collaboration Platform

  1. Function: Teams or Slack-style communication for back-channel agent questions, template location assistance, quality guidance
  2. Evidence: Slack log provided showing handler asking Policy & Quality Lead for template guidance; "Internal chat/Teams/Slack"

4.2 Core Business Systems

Guidewire Claims Center

  1. Function: System of record for claims data, case notes, documentation status, timeline tracking, coverage decisions
  2. Integration: Contact center agents and claims handlers manually log in to retrieve information
  3. Evidence: Interview transcript – "Guidewire Claims Center is the source of truth for everything claims-related."

SAP Billing

  1. Function: Payment history, outstanding balances, transaction details, refund status
  2. Integration: Manual queries by agents and billing specialists
  3. Evidence: "SAP Billing for payments, outstanding balances, and refunds."

Salesforce CRM

  1. Function: Customer master data, interaction history (limited), case tracking
  2. Limitation: Data reconciliation issues with Guidewire and SAP
  3. Evidence: Reference to multiple systems not reconciling data consistently

SharePoint

  1. Function: Template storage, document management, Claims Communications Playbook hosting
  2. Critical Issue: No version control workflow, multiple folders with ambiguous naming, archived and current files coexist, handlers maintain local copies
  3. Evidence: Slack log – "SharePoint is showing five folders" | "Because we have no versioning workflow."

4.3 Key Artifacts

Claims Communications Playbook v4.2

  1. Purpose: Defines eight mandatory components for decline and partial settlement letters: claim summary, facts, policy clause references, decision statement, reasoning, reconsideration rights, complaint rights, Ombudsman signposting
  2. Current State: Frequently violated in practice; audits identify consistent non-compliance
  3. Evidence: Playbook document provided

Customer Letter Templates

  1. Purpose: Pre-formatted letters for claim decisions
  2. Current State: Multiple versions exist ("2025 Pack – FINAL," "v2 – cleaner wording," "draft," "v1_temp"), naming inconsistent, handlers uncertain which to use
  3. Evidence: Slack and call transcript references to template confusion

Wrap Code List

  1. Purpose: Post-interaction categorization for reporting and analysis
  2. Current State: Extensive list, inconsistently applied, agents select first vaguely relevant option
  3. Business Impact: Poor intent data quality
  4. Evidence: "Intent data is poor due to inconsistent wrap codes"

Business Rules Documentation

  1. Current State: Partially codified; "judgment call" scenarios remain undefined
  2. Evidence: Need to identify "under-specified business rules" mentioned in discovery approach

5.0 Summary of Hand-offs and Accountability

5.1 Intra-Channel Hand-offs

IVR to Queue (Voice)

  1. Source: Automated IVR system
  2. Destination: ACD queue by line of business
  3. Context Transferred: Customer IVR selection (Claims / Policy & Servicing / Billing)
  4. Context Lost: No intent detail beyond category selection
  5. Accountability Gap: No differentiation of inquiry complexity
  6. Evidence: "Main number → IVR with 3 options → Single queue per IVR option"

Bot to Human Agent (Chat)

  1. Source: FAQ bot
  2. Destination: Contact center agent in digital queue
  3. Context Transferred: Last few conversation lines, possibly 1-2 captured entities
  4. Context Lost: Full conversation history, customer account context, prior interaction history
  5. Accountability Gap: Agent restarts inquiry process with minimal information
  6. Evidence: "Agent sees previous bot conversation; Minimal wrap coding"

5.2 Cross-Functional Hand-offs

Frontline Agent to Specialized Team (Warm Transfer)

  1. Source: Contact center agent
  2. Destination: Claims specialist, billing specialist, or complaints team
  3. Context Transferred: Verbal summary during live transfer
  4. Context Lost: No documented case history, no feedback on resolution to originating agent
  5. Accountability Gap: Originating agent cannot learn from outcome; no systematic improvement
  6. Evidence: "Live transfer to specialized team; No structured feedback loop"

Frontline Agent to Specialist (Async Case)

  1. Source: Contact center agent
  2. Destination: Back-office specialist team
  3. Context Transferred: Task/case documentation in system
  4. Context Lost: Customer tone, urgency, full context of prior interactions
  5. Accountability Gap: Originating agent has limited visibility into progress or resolution
  6. Evidence: "Agent creates internal task/case; Another team handles asynchronously; Limited visibility into resolution"

Claims Handler to Policy & Quality Lead

  1. Source: Claims handler
  2. Destination: Policy & Quality Lead
  3. Context Transferred: Draft letter, case details
  4. Context Lost: Handler uncertainty about correct template/clause not always documented
  5. Accountability Gap: Reactive quality review rather than proactive template governance
  6. Evidence: Call transcript showing handler requesting quality review: "I'll review your draft to make sure the explanation is consistent."

5.3 System-to-System Hand-offs (Data Flow)

Contact Center Platform to Guidewire Claims Center

  1. Flow: Agent manually navigates from contact center interface to Guidewire to retrieve claim data
  2. Fragmentation: No automated data pull; agent interprets and verbally explains to customer
  3. Accountability: Agent responsible for accurate interpretation
  4. Evidence: "Handlers manually log into Guidewire Claims Center, open PDF case notes, interpret information"

Contact Center Platform to SAP Billing

  1. Flow: Agent manually queries SAP to retrieve payment history and transaction details
  2. Fragmentation: No unified customer view combining claims and billing data
  3. Accountability: Agent responsible for data reconciliation if discrepancies exist
  4. Evidence: "SAP Billing for payments, outstanding balances, and refunds"

SharePoint to Local File Storage

  1. Flow: Handlers download templates from SharePoint, save local copies with personal edits
  2. Fragmentation: No centralized version control; handlers use inconsistent versions
  3. Accountability Gap: No oversight of which template version is actually used
  4. Evidence: Playbook Section 6.3 – "Handlers saving local copies of templates ('v2 – cleaner wording')"

5.4 Cross-Channel Hand-offs

Voice to Email / Email to Voice

  1. Context Transferred: None
  2. Context Lost: Complete prior interaction history
  3. Accountability Gap: Customer forced to re-explain issue; no channel orchestration
  4. Evidence: "Channels are siloed; no unified view of customer journeys"

Chat to Voice / Voice to Chat

  1. Context Transferred: None
  2. Context Lost: Complete prior interaction history
  3. Accountability Gap: Customer forced to re-explain issue; no channel orchestration
  4. Evidence: "Cross-Channel Gaps: Siloed channel operations; No unified customer view"

5.5 Critical Accountability Gaps Summary

Gap 1: No feedback mechanism from specialized teams to frontline agents on handoff outcomes prevents organizational learning and process improvement.

Gap 2: Data fragmentation across Guidewire, SAP, and Salesforce forces agents to make reconciliation decisions without clear escalation rules.

Gap 3: Template and clause reference version control is decentralized, creating regulatory compliance risk with no single point of accountability.

Gap 4: Cross-channel customer journeys are invisible to agents and management, preventing holistic experience optimization.

Gap 5: Bot-to-human escalations lose critical context, degrading service quality and extending handle times.

Gap 6: WFM planning occurs in isolation from AI deflection strategy, preventing optimized capacity allocation.

Document Conclusion

This current state documentation reveals a fundamentally manual, siloed contact center operation with significant process fragmentation at handoff points, poor intent data quality limiting optimization opportunities, and critical document management failures creating regulatory risk. The technology stack provides basic channel coverage but lacks intelligence in routing, context preservation, and cross-system integration. Addressing these gaps through AI agent transformation requires not only technology deployment but fundamental process redesign, data governance improvement, and human-AI workload integration.

Next Steps: Proceed to deep-dive discovery sessions with identified SMEs (Head of Claims Customer Service, Contact Center Operations Manager, Claims Policy & Quality Lead) to validate workflow details, quantify pain point impacts, and identify additional process workarounds requiring remediation before AI agent deployment.

Solution Design Template

0. Solution Selected

Automated Intent Capture System to address the Poor Intent Data Quality bottleneck occurring at Stage 1, Step 1.4 and Stage 2, Step 2.4 with Contact Center Agents using the Contact Center Platform.

Priority Justification: This solution is highest priority because poor intent data acts as a foundational blocker across the entire operation. Without accurate intent classification, the organization cannot effectively target automation opportunities (Goal 4), forecast contact volumes, optimize workforce planning (Goal 5), or strategically reduce contacts (Goal 1). The bottleneck affects every interaction and every downstream decision about automation investments. Additionally, speech/text analytics technology is mature and feasible to implement, offering high expected ROI by unlocking data-driven optimization across all other improvement initiatives. This solution removes the most critical data dependency blocking strategic automation progress.

1. Problem Statement

Contact Center Agents inconsistently apply wrap codes to customer interactions, making it impossible to understand true customer needs at scale or effectively target automation opportunities. This data quality failure prevents accurate forecasting by intent, causes misaligned automation investments targeting the wrong interaction types, and creates inaccurate WFM modeling that leads to overstaffing or understaffing. The inability to identify high-volume, automatable intents directly undermines strategic goals for contact reduction and automation rate improvement.

2. Goals & Success Metrics

Goal 1: Achieve 95%+ intent classification accuracy

  1. Metric: Agreement rate between automated classification and expert manual review

3. Scope

In Scope

  1. Deployment of speech analytics for voice interactions and text analytics for chat/email
  2. Creation of simplified intent taxonomy (30-40 categories vs. current 100+ wrap codes)
  3. Real-time intent classification during or immediately after customer interactions
  4. Mandatory validation workflow where agents confirm or correct automated intent classification
  5. Integration with Contact Center Platform, Chat Platform, and email systems
  6. Reporting dashboard showing intent volume trends, accuracy metrics, and automation opportunities
  7. Monthly intent taxonomy review process with Quality team
  8. Training for agents on new simplified intent validation workflow

Out of Scope

  1. Building custom analytics engine (will use vendor solution)
  2. Historical reclassification of interactions older than 90 days
  3. Intent-based routing implementation (separate future initiative)
  4. Automation of specific intents identified (those are downstream projects)
  5. Changes to underlying CRM data models beyond intent tagging
  6. Integration with external systems outside core contact center platforms

4. Current State vs Future State

Current State

  1. Agents manually select wrap codes from 100+ options with inconsistent definitions and overlapping categories
  2. No validation or quality control on wrap code selection; agents choose quickly to minimize after-call work time
  3. Intent data is unreliable for analysis; leadership relies on anecdotal evidence and small sample manual reviews
  4. Workforce Management forecasts by aggregate call volume only, unable to plan by interaction complexity or type
  5. Automation opportunities are identified through pilot testing or agent surveys rather than data-driven volume analysis
  6. No mechanism to track intent trends over time or correlate intents with customer satisfaction or resolution outcomes

Future State

  1. Speech and text analytics automatically classify customer intent during or immediately after interactions with 95%+ accuracy
  2. Agents validate automated classification using simplified taxonomy (30-40 clear categories) with mandatory selection
  3. Intent data populates real-time dashboards showing volume by category, trends, and accuracy metrics
  4. WFM forecasts by intent type, enabling skill-based staffing and complexity-aware capacity planning
  5. Automation prioritization driven by data: volume × automation feasibility × impact scores for each intent
  6. Monthly intent taxonomy governance ensures categories remain relevant, non-overlapping, and aligned with business strategy

5. Proposed Solution (Detailed)

Solution Architecture

Phase 1: Analytics Engine Deployment

  1. Deploy cloud-based speech analytics solution (e.g., NICE Nexidia, Verint, CallMiner) integrated with Contact Center Platform
  2. Deploy text analytics engine for chat and email channels with same intent taxonomy
  3. Configure real-time processing pipeline: interaction → automated classification → agent validation prompt → data warehouse

Phase 2: Intent Taxonomy Design

  1. Conduct 2-week discovery: analyze sample of 500 interactions across channels, interview 15 agents and 5 team leads
  2. Consolidate current 100+ wrap codes into 30-40 intent categories organized by:
  3. Customer journey stage (pre-claim, claim submission, claim status, payment, complaint, policy inquiry)
  4. Complexity tier (simple informational, moderate transactional, complex exception handling)
  5. Automation feasibility (high/medium/low based on data/system requirements)
  6. Define clear decision trees for each category with example phrases and exclusion criteria
  7. Validate taxonomy with Operations leadership and Quality team

Phase 3: Agent Validation Workflow

  1. After each interaction, system presents suggested intent category with confidence score
  2. Agent confirms or selects alternative from simplified pick-list (mandatory field to complete interaction)
  3. If agent changes classification, brief reason code captured for model improvement
  4. Validation step replaces current manual wrap code selection, targeting <30 seconds
  5. System logs both automated and agent-validated intents for accuracy tracking

Phase 4: Reporting & Governance

  1. Build Power BI dashboard showing:
  2. Intent volume trends (daily/weekly/monthly by category)
  3. Classification accuracy rates by category and by agent
  4. Top 20 intents ranked by volume with complexity and automation feasibility scores
  5. Forecast accuracy by intent type
  6. Establish monthly Intent Governance Review: Quality Lead + WFM + Automation PMO review taxonomy performance, identify new categories, archive obsolete ones
  7. Create automated alerts for intent volume anomalies (e.g., 20%+ increase in specific category week-over-week)

Technical Integration Points

  1. Contact Center Platform: Real-time event stream of interaction start/end, audio/text capture, wrap phase trigger
  2. Speech Analytics Engine: Audio processing, NLP classification, confidence scoring
  3. Text Analytics Engine: Chat/email body processing, intent classification
  4. Agent Desktop: Pop-up validation screen with suggested intent and pick-list
  5. Data Warehouse: Store interaction ID, automated intent, agent-validated intent, timestamps, confidence scores
  6. Reporting Layer: Power BI or Tableau connected to data warehouse

Required Artifacts

  1. Intent Taxonomy Reference Guide (definitions, examples, decision trees)
  2. Agent Quick Reference Card (simplified category list with keywords)
  3. Analytics Engine Configuration Playbook (model training, threshold tuning)
  4. Monthly Intent Governance Review Template
  5. Automation Opportunity Assessment Framework (scoring rubric for prioritization)

  2. Agents confirm the suggestion or select an alternative from a simplified list of 30-40 categories (vs. current 100+ wrap codes)

  4. Validation step takes <30 seconds vs. current manual wrap code search and selection

7. Implementation Plan

Phase 1: Pilot (Weeks 1-8)

Objectives

  1. Validate speech/text analytics accuracy in production environment
  2. Refine intent taxonomy based on real interaction data
  3. Train pilot group and gather feedback on validation workflow

Key Activities

  2. Week 3-4: Install and configure analytics engines; integrate with Contact Center Platform
  3. Week 4-5: Conduct intent taxonomy discovery workshops; design 30-40 category taxonomy
  4. Week 6: Deploy to pilot group of 20 agents (10% of workforce); shadow mode only (no mandatory validation yet)
  5. Week 7-8: Collect 2 weeks of classification data; conduct accuracy audit on 200-sample; refine model and taxonomy

  2. Technical Lead: IT Enterprise Architect
  3. Business Lead: Contact Center Operations Manager
  4. Quality Lead: responsible for taxonomy design and accuracy validation

  2. IT Infrastructure team capacity for integration work

Phase 2: Rollout (Weeks 9-16)

  2. Achieve 95% classification accuracy
  3. Transition to mandatory agent validation workflow

  2. Week 11: Enable mandatory validation workflow for pilot group; monitor adoption and handle time impact
  3. Week 12-14: Roll out to remaining agents in 3 waves (33% per week); provide real-time support via super users
  4. Week 15-16: Stabilize operations; tune model based on agent corrections; achieve 95% accuracy target

  2. Operations Manager: schedules rollout waves, monitors handle time and quality metrics

  2. Launch automation prioritization framework

  2. Week 18-20: WFM team builds intent-based forecasting models; validate against 4 weeks of actuals
  3. Week 21-22: Automation PMO publishes first ranked automation opportunity list; socialize with leadership
  4. Week 23-24: Review end-to-end performance; document lessons learned; plan next-generation capabilities (e.g., predictive intent on IVR)

Owners/Roles

  1. Quality Lead: facilitates monthly governance reviews
  2. WFM Manager: owns intent-based forecasting models
  3. Automation PMO: publishes automation opportunity reports
  4. Continuous Improvement team: analyzes trends and proposes taxonomy/process enhancements

  2. Executive buy-in for automation projects identified through intent data
  3. Resource allocation for WFM model development

8. Risks & Mitigations

Risk 1: Agent Resistance to New Validation Workflow

Description: Agents may perceive validation step as additional work or "Big Brother" monitoring, leading to non-compliance or rushed validations that undermine data quality.

Mitigation:

  1. Emphasize time savings from simplified taxonomy and system suggestions during training
  2. Show agents how their validation contributions improve future automation that reduces repetitive work
  3. Track and celebrate accuracy improvements; recognize agents with highest validation quality
  4. Ensure validation step replaces (not adds to) current wrap code workflow, targeting net neutral or reduced handle time

Risk 2: Speech Analytics Accuracy Below Target for Specific Interaction Types

Description: Model may struggle with accented speech, high-background-noise environments, or highly nuanced conversations, resulting in <95% accuracy for certain categories.

Mitigation:

  1. Conduct pre-deployment audio quality assessment; upgrade recording infrastructure if needed
  2. Identify low-confidence categories during pilot; provide additional model training data for those intents
  3. Implement confidence thresholds: flag low-confidence classifications (<80%) for mandatory agent review and reason code
  4. Plan for quarterly model retraining using agent corrections as ground truth data

Risk 3: Taxonomy Becomes Obsolete or Scope Creep

Description: Business needs evolve, new products launch, or stakeholders request overly granular categories, causing taxonomy to bloat back to 100+ categories or become misaligned with actual interaction patterns.

Mitigation:

  1. Establish monthly Intent Governance Review with cross-functional representation and decision authority
  2. Define clear criteria for adding new categories (minimum volume threshold, distinct from existing categories, actionable for automation or WFM)
  3. Archive obsolete categories rather than deleting to preserve historical trend analysis
  4. Enforce 30-40 category maximum through governance process; new additions require consolidation of existing categories

Risk 4: Data Warehouse or Reporting Infrastructure Cannot Handle Real-Time Volume

Description: Processing 50,000+ interactions/day with real-time classification and agent validation may exceed current data infrastructure capacity, causing delays or system outages.

Mitigation:

  1. Conduct infrastructure capacity assessment during vendor selection; rightsize data warehouse and processing pipelines
  2. Implement asynchronous processing where real-time is not critical (e.g., batch-process text analytics for emails overnight)
  3. Use cloud-based analytics platform with elastic scaling to handle volume spikes
  4. Establish performance monitoring with alerts for processing delays >2 minutes

Risk 5: Lack of Executive Commitment to Act on Intent Insights

Description: Organization invests in intent data infrastructure but leadership does not prioritize automation projects or WFM changes identified through the data, causing ROI failure.

Mitigation:

  1. Secure executive sponsorship from VP Operations before project launch; present business case with clear ROI projections
  2. Establish quarterly steering committee reviews showing intent trends, automation opportunities, and cost-saving potential
  3. Quick-win strategy: identify 1-2 high-volume, low-complexity intents for immediate automation in first 90 days to demonstrate value
  4. Tie automation roadmap funding decisions to intent volume data, making it the "single source of truth" for prioritization

9. Open Questions / Assumptions

Assumption: Budget of $200K-$400K [Assumption] is available for analytics platform licensing (20,000 interactions/month @ ~$0.15-0.30/interaction) plus implementation services.

Assumption: IT team has capacity to complete integration work within 8-week pilot phase. If constrained, timeline extends or requires external systems integrator.

Assumption: Agents currently spend 45-60 seconds on wrap activities; new validation workflow targets <30 seconds. Actual baseline handle time and wrap time need to be validated in Week 1.

Assumption: Current wrap code accuracy is 60-70% based on typical industry benchmarks for unstandardized agent selection. Should validate with manual audit before project launch.

Open Question: Does the organization have historical interaction recordings available for model training, or will training occur only on new interactions post-deployment (slower to reach accuracy targets)?

Open Question: What is the preferred analytics vendor? Need to complete vendor evaluation by Week 2 to maintain timeline.

Open Question: Are there regulatory or compliance requirements (e.g., GDPR, PCI-DSS) for storing and processing interaction audio/text that may constrain analytics platform deployment model (cloud vs. on-premise)?

Open Question: How will intent data be shared with downstream systems (e.g., CRM, Guidewire Claims Center) for use in other process improvement initiatives?

10. Acceptance Criteria

  1. Speech and text analytics engines deployed and integrated with Contact Center Platform, Chat Platform, and email systems with <2-minute processing latency for 95% of interactions
  2. Intent taxonomy finalized with 30-40 categories, documented with clear definitions, examples, and decision trees, and approved by Operations, Quality, WFM, and Automation PMO stakeholders
  3. Automated intent classification achieves 95% accuracy (agreement with expert manual review) across all categories within 90 days of full deployment
  4. Agent validation workflow deployed to 100% of agents; mandatory intent confirmation taking <30 seconds on average and integrated into wrap phase
  5. Real-time reporting dashboard available showing intent volume trends, classification accuracy by category, and top 20 automation opportunities, updated daily
  6. WFM forecasting accuracy improves from ±25% to ±10% variance [Assumption] within 6 months, using intent-level forecasts
  7. Monthly Intent Governance Review process established with cross-functional participation, first 3 reviews completed, and taxonomy updated based on findings
  8. Automation PMO publishes ranked list of top 10 automation opportunities based on intent volume data, with ROI projections and feasibility scores, within 60 days of go-live

Risks

Risk 1: Automating Broken Processes

Description: AI agent may amplify existing process dysfunction if deployed on top of workarounds, siloed data, or under-specified business rules.

Mitigation: Conduct thorough current state mapping with SMEs to identify red flags: Access databases/Excel trackers, incentive conflicts, undefined "judgment call" scenarios. Prioritize process redesign before automation.

Source: Discovery Call [16:00] - "Otherwise we'll just get faster at being wrong."

Risk 2: Agent Resistance and Union Opposition

Description: Contact center agents may perceive AI tools as surveillance or job threat, leading to adoption resistance and union grievances.

Mitigation: Frame initiative as capability enhancement, provide visible "accept/edit/reject" controls, measure and publish agent empowerment metrics, involve union leadership early.

Source: Discovery Call [18:10] - "Our contact center is unionized... I don't want to announce a program that sounds like 'Project Fewer People.'"

Risk 3: Regulatory Rejection

Description: Insufficient explainability, incomplete audit trails, or perceived customer unfairness could trigger regulatory intervention or mandate rollback.

Mitigation: Implement comprehensive interaction logging, involve Chief Risk & Compliance Officer in design reviews, prepare demonstration materials for regulatory confidence building.

Source: Discovery Call [20:05] - "If they leave nodding, we've done it right."

Risk 4: Data Quality and System Integration Failures

Description: Data inconsistencies between Guidewire, SAP, and Salesforce could cause customer-facing errors, eroding trust in AI agent accuracy.

Mitigation: Implement strict data reconciliation logic with escalation on discrepancy, establish Guidewire and SAP as golden records, conduct integration testing before customer-facing deployment.

Source: Discovery Call [10:15] - "If data doesn't reconcile, the agent escalates instead of guessing."

Risk 5: Scope Creep into Sales or Complex Claims

Description: Pressure to expand AI agent into sales inquiries or fully automated claim denials could introduce unacceptable risk or channel conflict.

Mitigation: Enforce documented non-goals, require executive re-approval for scope expansion beyond phase 1 journeys, maintain human-in-the-loop for high-stakes decisions.

Risk 6: Competitive Timing Pressure

Mitigation: Balance speed-to-market with proper governance checkpoints, phase rollout starting with lower-risk claim status journey, establish clear success criteria before expansion.

2) Transcript – MC & Contact Center Operations Manager (Liam)

Google Meet – Existing Process Deep Dive: Contact Center Operations
 Participants:

  1. Mark Chen (MC) – VP Customer Operations & Transformation Lead
  2. Liam Patel (LP) – Contact Center Operations Manager

[00:05] – Join & Framing

MC: Hey Liam, thanks for joining. All good on audio?

LP: Yep, all good.

MC: Great. Today I want to stay out of PowerPoint land and get into how your contact center actually works — routing, queues, metrics, all the plumbing that an AI agent would have to sit in.

I’m going to ask dumb questions, assume I know nothing.

LP: Perfect, that’s my favourite kind of session.

1. Channel & Queue Structure

[01:00]

MC: Let’s start with channels. What do you run today?

LP: We’ve got three main customer-facing channels:

  1. Voice – the traditional call center.
  2. Chat – from the website and the customer portal.
  3. Email – which is more like shared inboxes managed by back-office teams.

From a routing perspective, voice and chat are handled in the contact center platform; email is still semi-manual.

MC: And how are the voice queues set up?

LP: Very simply, too simply if I’m honest:

  1. Main number hits a generic greeting and then a menu:
    1. 1 = Claims
    2. 2 = Policies & Servicing
    3. 3 = Billing & Payments
  2. Each option dumps the customer into a single queue per line of business.
  3. Within that queue, any agent with the right skill set can pick up the call — we don’t differentiate between “where is my claim” and “I want to complain.”

MC: So no fine-grained intent routing?

LP: Exactly. We treat “claims” as one bucket.

2. Chat & Bot Hand-Off

[03:00]

MC: What about chat? How does that work?

LP: For chat:

  1. A customer opens a chat window on the site.
  2. We have a very basic bot that can answer FAQs: opening hours, how to report a claim, what documents we usually need.
  3. As soon as anything becomes account-specific — “What’s my balance?” “Where is my claim?” — the bot escalates to a human.

The escalation is effectively: one big digital queue. We don’t do smart routing by intent or complexity.

MC: And when it hands off, does the agent get context?

LP: Minimal. They see:

  1. The last few lines of the conversation,
  2. And maybe one or two entities the bot captured.

But they don’t get a full “customer profile plus journey.” It’s like picking up halfway through a chat without the backstory.

3. Metrics & WFM

[05:00]

MC: Let’s talk metrics. How do you measure the contact center today?

LP: We’re quite traditional:

  1. Service Level – 80/20 target on voice.
  2. Average Handle Time (AHT) – per queue.
  3. Abandonment Rate – keep it under 5–7%.
  4. A bit of CSAT from post-call surveys, but it’s not a strong driver.

We don’t reliably track First Contact Resolution (FCR) by intent, because wrap codes are all over the place.

MC: How does Workforce Management fit into that?

LP: WFM forecasts volume and AHT by queue and half-hour interval, then builds schedules. We do some intraday adjustments, but it’s basically fixed staffing against historical patterns.

We don’t yet model “AI handling X% of intent A, Y% of intent B” — we treat bot volume as a small deflection.

4. Pain Points With the Current Setup

[07:30]

MC: From your seat, what are the main operational pain points in the current model?

LP: Three big ones:

  1. Crude routing.
    1. All claims calls are treated the same. A simple “where is my claim” and a complex complaint hit the same agents, in the same queue.
    2. That kills efficiency and also frustrates our best agents.
  2. Messy intent data.
    1. After the call, agents pick a wrap code from a long list. Many choose the first vaguely relevant option.
    2. That means our reporting on “what are people calling about” is imprecise, so it’s hard to target automation.
  3. Bots as friction, not relief.
    1. The current bot can’t take actions; it only answers static FAQs.
    2. Customers tend to repeat themselves when they reach a human — “I already told the bot this.”
    3. So we’ve added a step without reducing effort.

MC: All right. So from an AI-agents point of view, we’d want to (1) capture true intent, (2) route based on that, and (3) let agents — human or AI — actually do things, not just respond.

LP: Exactly.

5. Where You’d See Agentification Helping Most

[10:00]

MC: If we come in with AI agents, where do you see the biggest operational leverage?

LP: One: front-door triage.

  1. Use AI to understand what the customer wants in their own words.
  2. Route “simple, transactional stuff” to an AI agent that can handle it end-to-end.
  3. Route complex or emotional issues to humans with the right skills.

Two: pre-populating context for human agents.

  1. Before a call or chat lands, pull up the claim, recent contacts, outstanding docs, and show a one-page summary.
  2. That alone could shave 30–60 seconds off many calls.

Three: better measurement.

  1. If AI can automatically tag intent reliably, WFM and planning get much better.

MC: And what are you nervous about?

LP: Two things:

  1. If we don’t change our KPIs, agents might game the system — e.g., dumping things on the AI to keep their AHT low.
  2. If AI is bolted on without changing routing logic, we’ll have a fancy bot sitting next to a legacy IVR, and nothing will really change.

6. Constraints & “Don’t Break This”

[13:00]

MC: Any hard constraints we should know about? Things we mustn’t break?

LP: Yes:

  1. Our unions are sensitive about job security. I’m okay with AI handling volume, but we can’t frame it as “replace agents.”
  2. We mustn’t deteriorate Service Level while experimenting. If we shift too much volume badly, queues explode.
  3. Integrations: we need to work within the capabilities of the telephony platform. Wholesale replacement is not on the table near term.

MC: Got it. So we design AI agents assuming they coexist with the current stack, but orchestrate as much as possible.

LP: Right.

MC: This is super useful. Thanks, Liam.

LP: Anytime.
